issues.description: solution.py contains comment '# print("2025 is my year!")' — hardcoded year reference that is already outdated.
issues.suggestedFix: Update to a generic year-free phrase or use dynamic year.
issues.webResearchNote: Current year is 2026. The hardcoded 2025 reference is already outdated. Either use a timeless phrase or generate the year dynamically.

issues.description: 04-key_point.md says 'You can use web-based Python environments (playgrounds) to write and run code without installing anything' but the lesson content discusses Code Tutor's built-in editor and local Python installation — never mentions web-based environments.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to 'You can use Code Tutor's built-in editor to write and run code' or remove the web-based reference.

issues.description: 04-key_point.md mentions 'The asterisk (*) means multiplication in Python' but asterisk/multiplication is not taught in this lesson — it appears in Lesson 04 with string multiplication. Forward reference without context.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove this bullet or add '(you'll see this soon)'.

issues.description: 01-theory.md provides uv install via 'curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh' which is a Unix command. Since Code Tutor is a WPF (Windows) desktop app, Windows-specific install instructions would be more appropriate for the primary audience.
issues.suggestedFix: Add Windows install: 'powershell -c "irm https://astral.sh/uv/install.ps1 | iex"' or mention the Windows installer.

issues.description: Title 'Making the Computer Talk' implies OUTPUT, but the lesson actually introduces INPUT (input()) and variables. Title/content mismatch. Lesson 04 is 'Listening to the User' (implies input) but teaches f-strings (output formatting). The two titles appear conceptually swapped.
issues.suggestedFix: Rename to 'Having a Conversation' or 'Your First Input' — or swap titles with Lesson 04.

issues.description: 02-example.md has a stray trailing empty code block (lone triple-backtick at the very end with no content or language specifier).
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the trailing empty code fence.

issues.description: 04-key_point.md mentions input('Your question here: ') prompt-inside-input syntax, but the lesson's own example code uses the two-line pattern (print() then input()). The prompt-inside-input syntax is taught in Lesson 04.
issues.suggestedFix: Add '(we'll use this shortcut in the next lesson)' or remove.

issues.description: 02-example.md expected output shows literal '{city}' in the line 'Welcome, Jordan from {city}!' instead of the substituted value 'Seattle'. The f-string in the code f'\nWelcome, {name} from {city}!' would produce 'Welcome, Jordan from Seattle!'. This will confuse learners about how f-strings work.
issues.suggestedFix: Change expected output line to 'Welcome, Jordan from Seattle!'.

issues.description: challenge.json common mistake #3 lists print(f'Hello {name}') as wrong, but this is perfectly valid Python f-string syntax. Teaching correct code as incorrect will mislead beginners.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove this entry or replace with an actual common mistake, e.g., forgetting the 'f' prefix: print('Hello {name}') showing literal {name}.

issues.description: Title 'Listening to the User' implies input-focused content, but the main new concepts are f-strings and string multiplication (output formatting). The title doesn't reflect the actual teaching focus.
issues.suggestedFix: Consider title swap with Lesson 03 or rename to 'Smooth Conversations with F-strings'.

issues.description: challenge.json common mistake #3 states 'Bad: Ask for age, then just print the age' with consequence 'This will cause an error' — but this would NOT cause an error; it's merely poor practice.
issues.suggestedFix: Change consequence to 'This works but doesn't demonstrate calculations or add value'.

issues.description: 04-key_point.md uses inline HTML/CSS for a congratulations box (p class='congratulations-box' style=...) which may not render consistently across all markdown renderers.
issues.suggestedFix: Consider using standard markdown formatting (e.g., blockquote with emoji) for portability.
